Where the other descents are called partial, a portion or a ray of God, of Kṛṣṇa alone it says *kṛṣṇas tu bhagavān svayam*, “but Kṛṣṇa is God himself.” Bryant notes that this half line “becomes something of a *mahāvākya*,” a great utterance, for the Kṛṣṇa sects, and that the sixteenth century theologians, Vallabha and Caitanya, took it to its logical end and held that it is “not Kṛṣṇa who is an incarnation of Viṣṇu but Viṣṇu who is a partial manifestation of Kṛṣṇa.” The cowherd who began as the junior partner ends up swallowing the senior. A tradition that can flip its own hierarchy and keep going for centuries is not something brittle or freshly bolted together.

### The Buddha, and the brother he replaced

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The ninth descent is the showpiece of the whole syncretic machinery, and also the place where two of the traditions in our corpus openly disagree. In the standard Puranic list Viṣṇu is born as the Buddha, and in the orthodox framing he does it in order to *delude* the impious into abandoning the Vedas, so that they can be destroyed, a backhanded canonization of the rival founder as a deliberate divine deception. The identification is firmly datable: Hindus came to regard the Buddha as a descent of Viṣṇu only between about 450 and the sixth century CE, first in the Viṣṇu Purāṇa. Jaiswal, against the cynical reading, argues that the move was not a brahmin trick but a popular rapprochement, that “the identification of the Buddha with Nārāyaṇa was not the result of a clever machination on the part of the brāhmaṇas,” noting that the Buddhist Lalitavistara itself already calls the Buddha Nārāyaṇa and Mahāpuruṣa.

And here is a detail that says everything about how constructed the famous ten really are. In the Śrī Vaiṣṇava tradition that Chari represents, the ninth descent is **not the Buddha at all**. Chari’s list of ten names, in the ninth slot, **Balarāma**, the elder brother, and omits the Buddha entirely. The Buddha had to be inserted into the popular list around the sixth century, displacing Balarāma, and a major theological school never accepted the substitution. The “canonical ten” are canonical only by sect and by century. There is no fixed, eternal, agreed list, and the fact that one tradition worships Balarāma where another worships the Buddha in the same numbered slot is the proof.

### Kalki, the rider to come

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The tenth descent has not happened. At the end of the present dark age, the Kali Yuga, Viṣṇu will be born as Kalki, a Brahmin of the village of Śambhala, and ride out on a white horse with a blazing sword to destroy the wicked and restore the age of truth. The figure is in the Mahābhārata’s forest book and elaborated in the Purāṇas. Jaiswal suggests his conception “may have been inspired by the idea of Maitreya, the Buddha of the future,” and that in the Purāṇas he becomes “the symbol of the ultimate triumph of brahmanism.” Chari lists him, at number thirty eight of the thirty nine, as “the form of Viṣṇu riding over a horse, yet to take place at the end of the Kali yuga,” whose purpose is “re establishing the lost dharma.” A future descent is a useful thing for a religion to keep in reserve, and it has been claimed by more than one ambitious man across the centuries.

### What the ten really are

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Step back from the individual stories and a clear shape appears, and it is the shape of the whole essay in miniature. The fish, the tortoise, and the boar are cosmogonic acts of Prajāpati, the creator, annexed to Viṣṇu in the Puranic period; the man lion and the axe Rāma are late absorptions of fierce local or sectarian gods, the one carrying the old Indra and Namuci plot, the other a caste charter; the dwarf alone was always Viṣṇu’s, the Ṛgvedic strider in narrative dress; Rāma and Kṛṣṇa are deified heroes, the human descents that drove the doctrine; the Buddha is a sixth century polemical annexation that one major school never accepted; and Kalki is the apocalypse held in reserve. The ten are not a single ancient revelation. They are the sediment of a religion absorbing the gods around it across two thousand years, with the oldest layer, the dwarf, reaching back to the Bronze Age strider with whom we began.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part Six. The Long Afterlife

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The story does not stop at the Gupta temples. If the first thousand years of Viṣṇu’s history is the slow fusion of the Vedic strider, the cosmic Nārāyaṇa, and the Vṛṣṇi hero, the next thousand is the long flowering of devotion to the god they became. It is here that the bhakti the colonial scholars wanted to credit to Christianity actually unfolds, out of entirely Indian roots, into the religion a third of India still practises. Bhandarkar’s 1913 book remains the clearest map of it, and most of what follows is his.

### The temple of love, and the god who is feared

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ljZj!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F82f648ff-e68d-4e06-8a0c-4e56d3d9a4fe_1074x1464.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ljZj!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F82f648ff-e68d-4e06-8a0c-4e56d3d9a4fe_1074x1464.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Seal representing Vishnu blessing a worshiper, Gandhara, 4th-6th century CE. It was found in what was then India, now Khyber Pakhtunkhwa in Pakistan. The inscription in Bactrian reads: The leader of worship.

Bhandarkar begins with a psychological observation that organizes everything. Of the two Vedic gods who survived into supreme status, Viṣṇu and Rudra-Śiva, the one is approached through love and the other through fear. “What contributed to the formation of Vaiṣṇavism,” he writes, “were the appearances and occurrences which excited love, admiration and a spirit of worship; while in Rudra-Śaivism the sentiment of fear is at the bottom.” And he puts it in a single sentence that is hard to improve on: “in India the god that is loved is Viṣṇu-Nārāyaṇa-Vāsudeva-Kṛṣṇa, while the god that is feared is Rudra-Śiva.” Vaiṣṇavism is, at its core, the religion of the God who came down to be loved.

The word for that love, *bhakti*, is older than its later passion. Bhandarkar’s philology is careful: the term “in the sense of love is not to be found except in a verse” of the Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad, and even in Rāmānuja’s hands “Bhakti means constant meditation and corresponds to the Upāsana of the Upaniṣads.” The word “etymologically signifies resorting to and then loving the thing resorted to.” The thing came before the word: the love of the soul for the Supreme “was an idea implied and often expressed by the word *priya*, dear, in the Upaniṣad period.” Devotion grew out of the Upaniṣads, not out of any foreign import.

### The Āḻvārs and the Tamil Veda

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The emotional flood begins in the Tamil south, with the poet saints called the **Āḻvārs**, “those immersed in God,” twelve of them, whom Bhandarkar redates from the fantastic traditional figures to “about the fifth or sixth century” and after, though current scholarship tends to place the group a little later, across roughly the sixth to ninth centuries. Their Tamil hymns, gathered into the four thousand verses of the *Nālāyira Divya Prabandham*, are “considered as very sacred and spoken of as the Vaiṣṇava Veda.” Here, for the first time, God is sung to in the language of the people and in the accents of helpless love, and the songs drove Buddhism and Jainism out of the Tamil country. Vasudha Narayanan, in Bryant’s sourcebook, makes the point that for this tradition the Tamil hymns stand beside the Sanskrit Veda as a second revelation, the *ubhaya Vedānta*, the dual scripture.

### The Ācāryas, and the war over Śaṅkara

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Āḻvārs supplied the feeling; the **Ācāryas** supplied the philosophy, and they did it under pressure. By the eighth century the great Advaitin Śaṅkara had taught that the world is illusion and the personal God a lower truth dissolved, at the summit, into an attributeless absolute. To a religion built on love between a real soul and a real God, this was, in Bhandarkar’s words, “considered as destructive of the Bhakti.” The whole later history of Vaiṣṇava thought is the building of theologies that could hold devotion and metaphysics together against Śaṅkara’s world-illusion.

The line begins with **Nāthamuni**, who gathered the Āḻvār hymns, and his successor **Yāmuna**, who, unable “to maintain the doctrine of Bhakti in the face of the theory of Advaita,” directed his own successor to answer Śaṅkara on his own ground, in a commentary on the Brahma Sūtras. That successor was **Rāmānuja**, born by the traditional reckoning around 1017, who lived and taught at Śrīraṅgam, was persecuted by a Śaiva Chola king, and built the system called **Viśiṣṭādvaita**, qualified non dualism. Its elegance is that it makes the world and the souls real without making them separate from God: “the individual soul and the insensate world are the attributes of the Supreme Soul. They constitute his body, just as the body and the indwelling soul constitute the human being.” God is one, but a one with a real body of real souls, loved and loving. Rāmānuja’s bhakti, Bhandarkar notes, “is reduced to the form of a continuous meditation,” the disciplined upāsanā of the Upaniṣads, and to it he joined **prapatti**, self surrender, the throwing of oneself on God’s grace, which “can be practised by all orders, including Śūdras.” His followers later split over how that grace works, the famous quarrel of the monkey and the cat: does the soul cling to God as a baby monkey grips its mother, with some effort of its own, or does God seize the soul as a cat carries its kitten, by grace alone. It is a real theological fault line, and it is the kind a religion only develops when it has been thinking hard about love for a very long time.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!3ERW!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd48647cd-b918-414b-8500-d8320320f6f1_960x1338.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!3ERW!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd48647cd-b918-414b-8500-d8320320f6f1_960x1338.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Ramanujacharya, who propounded the philosophy of Vishishtadvaita Vedanta

After Rāmānuja the schools multiply, each giving a different answer to the same question of how the soul and God are one and not one. **Madhva**, in the thirteenth century, found even Rāmānuja too monist and taught outright **dualism**, *Dvaita*, with five eternal and unbridgeable distinctions between God, souls, and matter; his supreme name is mostly Viṣṇu, and Bhandarkar notes that in his hands “the Gopāla-Kṛṣṇa element seems to be entirely absent,” the religion swung back from the cowherd toward the cosmic God. **Nimbārka** taught identity in difference and gave Kṛṣṇa and his beloved **Rādhā** an “almost exclusive prominence.” **Rāmānanda** carried the movement north into the vernacular, worshipping Rāma and Sītā and making “no distinction between Brāhmaṇas and members of the degraded castes,” and from his circle came **Kabīr**, the Muslim weaver who preached a stark imageless monotheism with the name of Rāma, and, later, **Tulsīdās**, whose Hindi *Rāmcaritmānas* became the Bible of the north. **Vallabha**, around 1500, built the path of the divine child, **Puṣṭimārga**, the way of grace, around the boy Kṛṣṇa of Braj, with daily temple worship that wakes, bathes, dresses, feeds, and cradles the god as an infant. And in Bengal, his contemporary **Caitanya** took the emotional road to its end, teaching that the highest love, when it reaches its peak, “constitutes itself into Rādhā,” and losing himself in the ecstatic chanting of the divine names.

### The late making of Rādhā

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Here the honest historian has to mark a late arrival, because it bears directly on the antiquity argument. **Rādhā**, the beloved who dominates the later imagination, is a genuinely late creation. She “is not mentioned by name in the Harivaṃśa, Viṣṇu Purāṇa and the Bhāgavata,” the very texts that tell the cowherd Kṛṣṇa’s story. She emerges fully only around the sixteenth century, in the Nārada Pāñcarātra and the theologies of Nimbārka, Vallabha, and Caitanya, spun out of a hint in the Bhāgavata’s account of the one favoured gopī. Bhandarkar, a Victorian reformer, disapproved, calling her elevation “a degrading element.” You need not share his disapproval to accept his chronology. The romantic theology of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa is medieval. It is the furthest flower on the tree, and nobody should mistake it for the root.

### The centre never moves

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Two thousand years of this, and the centre still does not move. The religion never stops elaborating: the Tamil hymns, the six rival Vedāntas, the boy of Braj, the saint of Bengal, the Maratha poets of the sober god Viṭṭhala who “discarded Rādhā-Kṛṣṇaism” for a purer devotion. And yet, as Bhandarkar says, through all of it “Vāsudeva as the name of the Supreme Being stands in the background of all,” and the spiritual core is “essentially derived from the Bhagavadgītā.” The bhakti that a missionary scholar in 1840 wanted to credit to Christianity is, when you actually trace it, the flowering of a devotion whose first clear statement is the Gītā, whose vocabulary is Upaniṣadic, and whose object is the Vedic Viṣṇu. It grew from inside, over three thousand years, from a Bronze Age root.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Part Seven. The Case, and the Quarrel

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

We have followed the god through his three ages: the Bronze Age deity, the worship of the last centuries BCE, the late system. The evidence for the first two has been laid out along the way, in the hymns and the inscriptions, and I will not relist it here. Two things remain, and they are the two I promised at the start: to show that the argument does not depend on me, and to ask how the opposite impression ever became the default.

### Where even the opponents agree

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The strongest form of the argument does not lean on any single school. It rests on the points where schools that agree on nothing else converge, because those are the claims that no ideological lens can be accused of manufacturing.

There are at least four such points. First, that Viṣṇu’s identification with the sacrifice, *yajño vai viṣṇuḥ*, is the hinge of his rise: the colonial Macdonell notes it, the structuralist Gonda builds on it, the materialist Jaiswal cites it, the traditionalist Chari claims it as Vedic, and the comparativist Manasataramgini turns it into a measurable curve. Second, that the Heliodoros pillar proves a foreigner-attested cult of Vāsudeva in the second century BCE: Bhandarkar, Jaiswal, Majumdar, and Chari all build on it. Third, that the developed system, the vyūha metaphysics and the fixed list of descents, is a later crystallization; even Chari, the eternalist, concedes that the vyūha doctrine is a Pāñcarātra development absent from the Gītā. Fourth, that the oldest descents, the dwarf and the boar, sit in the late Vedic Brāhmaṇa layer, which Macdonell, Keith, the epigraphist D. C. Sircar, and Manasataramgini all grant.

And the device that makes these land hardest is to let each concession come from the school least inclined to give it. The Marxist Jaiswal, whose whole book places the formation of Vaiṣṇavism in the last centuries BCE and the first CE, nonetheless writes of Ṛgvedic Viṣṇu that “even in the Ṛgvedic age he was considered to be a great god,” and “he may have been more popular among the masses whose ideas and inclinations are not recorded in the Vedas.” She even sides with Gonda’s pervasion reading over the old solar one. The author of the standard “late” book concedes the seed and the argument from silence in a single breath. The traditional theologian Chari, who would prefer everything eternal, concedes that the vyūha is later. The nationalist Majumdar, who defends Hindu antiquity at every turn, concedes that the cowherd Kṛṣṇa is late. Each admission is worth more for coming from the witness with the most reason to withhold it.

### Why we were taught otherwise

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

If the evidence runs this way, why is the reverse the default? The answer is partly about the lenses through which the question has been asked, and naming them is the point.

The simplest distortion is the hymn count itself. To call Viṣṇu “minor” because he has few Ṛgvedic hymns quietly carries a second claim, that he was therefore unimportant. The first does not entail the second, and the scholars who know the text best say so.

The deeper distortion is older and uglier, and we met it earlier: the missionary scholarship of the nineteenth century, Wilson studying Hinduism to refute it, Weber deriving Kṛṣṇa devotion from Christianity, a framing built to make the tradition look late and borrowed. That theory was dismantled a century ago by Bhandarkar, Chanda, and Raychaudhuri, using the very inscriptions we have been reading, and even Jaiswal records that it is now abandoned. It survives in the popular afterglow, not in the scholarship.

The most respectable and most influential distortion is the modern one, and it is subtle because it is not a mistake so much as a choice. The dominant school of twentieth century Indian history, the materialist tradition of Kosambi, Sharma, and Jaiswal, reads religion as a reflex of social and economic life. Its instinct is to foreground what can be dated and attached to a social formation, so it trains its attention on the period when the cult was systematized, brahmanized, given temples and texts and a settled theology, the last centuries BCE and the first CE, and it leaves the undatable Vedic root in shadow. We saw the method at its purest in the etymology of bhakti, where devotion is traced to the sharing out of tribal food, and in the reading of every Vaiṣṇava element as an originally separate tribal or totemic cult, the boar a pig totem, Narasiṃha a dreadful Punjab god, Saṃkarṣaṇa a serpent of the furrows, Śrī a fertility goddess, the cowherd Kṛṣṇa an Ābhīra deity, all absorbed into a brahmanical synthesis. This is a powerful method, and it has produced real knowledge. But a method is a lens, and no lens is neutral. One built to illuminate the datable synthesis will show you the datable synthesis, and then the synthesis starts to look like an origin.

And underneath all three distortions lies the single confusion this essay began with. *Systematization is not origin.* The lateness of the system, the vyūha metaphysics, the fixed ten, the divine marriage, the schools, is not in dispute; even Chari, from the opposite extreme, grants it. What is in dispute is only whether the system is the same thing as the beginning. The frequency reading, the colonial frame, and the materialist lens all, in their different ways, answer yes. The evidence answers no.

### What has to change

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Let me be precise about the claim, because precision is the whole point, and because the careless version of this argument does real damage to the careful one.

Keep the scholarly date for the system. The four fold emanation, the canonical ten, the divine couple, the great temples of the Gupta age and after, all of that really is the work of roughly 200 BCE to 500 CE and later. No honest case requires moving it, and the careful gopī romance, the consort goddess, the Buddha in the ninth slot, the systematic theology of the descents, all of it is genuinely young.

What has to change is the slide from there to the impression that Viṣṇu and the devotion to him are correspondingly recent. They are not. The deity is Bronze Age, the organized worship is more than two thousand years old, and only the system is late. To date the religion from the age of its theology is to date the Nile from the year of its first dam. The river was already running, for a very long time, before anyone built the thing we can put a date on.

Return, at the end, to Heliodoros and his pillar. The Greek who knelt to Vāsudeva around 113 BCE was not present at a birth. He was a latecomer to something already ancient, systematic enough to have an ethics he could quote, confident enough to draw a convert from across the Hellenistic world. He could become a Bhāgavata because the tradition he joined had not begun in his century, or anywhere near it.

And the pillar is still there. It stands on the riverbank near Vidiśā, and the fishing communities along the water still smear it with vermilion and call it Khamba Baba, the pillar-saint, and pray to it for the birth of a son. A Greek ambassador’s column to “the god of gods,” two thousand years on, is still a place where Indians come to worship. That is the real span of the thing. Follow it back as far as the evidence runs and you do not reach a beginning two thousand years ago. You reach a Bronze Age poet singing of the god who paced out the world in three strides and kept a wellspring of honey in his highest heaven, the god whose footstep the wise have always watched, stretched out like the eye in the sky.

The tradition kept the threads, even when it forgot it was keeping them. When the Mahābhārata wanted to name the supreme God, it gave him a thousand names, and the litany of the thousand, still chanted every day in Vaiṣṇava homes, opens with two of them: *viśvaṃ viṣṇur vaṣaṭkāraḥ*. The universe. Viṣṇu. The ritual call. In the first breath of his own thousand named hymn the god is the all, the pervader, and the cry of the sacrifice. Those are the two things this essay has followed from the Bronze Age forward: the one who strides through everything, and the one who is the rite that holds everything together. Both names are older than the religion is usually said to be.

This is not a recent religion. It is one of the oldest objects of continuous human devotion that we can actually name and date, and it is long past time our standard account of it said so.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!JUse!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F167bb536-0aae-476f-a343-f8d16380ba53_3212x2279.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!JUse!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F167bb536-0aae-476f-a343-f8d16380ba53_3212x2279.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Here, Vishnu sleeps on the coils of Ananta, the World Snake. He will awaken for the next cycle of creation, which heralds the destruction of all things. From southern India**, circa 14th century CE.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Sources and further reading

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**Primary texts**

- **Ṛgveda**, trans. Stephanie Jamison and Joel Brereton. Especially
  1.154, 1.155, and 1.22 (the three strides and the highest step), 7.99
  and 7.100, the Vṛtra alliance verse 8.100.12, the Puruṣa Sūkta 10.90,
  and the riddle hymn 1.164 (the navel of existence).

- **White Yajurveda (Vājasaneyi Saṃhitā), Sāmaveda, and Atharvaveda**,
  trans. R. T. H. Griffith. For “Viṣṇu, that is, the sacrifice,” the
  Viṣṇu stride rite, the “loftiest place of Viṣṇu,” and the womb shaping
  charm (Atharvaveda 5.25).

- **Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa**, trans. Julius Eggeling. For “the sacrifice is
  Viṣṇu,” the dwarf, Nārāyaṇa, and the Puruṣamedha.

- **Taittirīya Saṃhitā**, trans. A. B. Keith. For the dwarf, the boar,
  and Prajāpati.

- **Aitareya Brāhmaṇa.** For “Agni is the lowest, Viṣṇu the highest.”

- **Bhagavad Gītā**, Winthrop Sargeant’s word by word edition. Chapters
  4, 7, 9, 10, 11, and 18.

- **Mahābhārata**, trans. Bibek Debroy (unabridged). The Nārāyaṇīya and
  the Śvetadvīpa episode (Śānti Parva), the Śiśupāla scene (Sabhā
  Parva), the Nara-Nārāyaṇa formula, and the Viṣṇu-sahasranāma
  (Anuśāsana Parva).

- **Bhāgavata Purāṇa.** 5.26.31 (the hell of the human sacrificers) and
  1.3.28 (*kṛṣṇas tu bhagavān svayam*).

- **Inscriptions** in D. C. Sircar, *Select Inscriptions Bearing on
  Indian History and Civilization*. The Besnagar (Heliodoros), Ghosundi,
  Nānāghāṭ, and Mora records.

**Scholarship**

- A. A. Macdonell, *Vedic Mythology*.

- Jan Gonda, *Aspects of Early Viṣṇuism* — the strides, the sacrifice,
  the navel and Padmanābha, the avatāras, kingship, Śrī, and the
  pervasiveness thesis.

- F. B. J. Kuiper, *Ancient Indian Cosmogony*.

- R. N. Dandekar, *Vedic Mythological Tracts*.

- R. G. Bhandarkar, *Vaiṣṇavism, Śaivism and Minor Religious Systems* —
  the three stream model, the bhakti philology, the Āḻvārs and Ācāryas
  from Rāmānuja to Caitanya, the loved Viṣṇu and feared Śiva, and the
  late making of Rādhā.

- Suvira Jaiswal, *The Origin and Development of Vaiṣṇavism*.

- Edwin Bryant (ed.), *Krishna: A Sourcebook*; and Bryant, *The Quest
  for the Origins of Vedic Culture* — the early Vāsudeva chain, the
  Bhāgavata reversal, and the Mitanni evidence.

- R. C. Majumdar and others, *The History and Culture of the Indian
  People* (the Vedic Age, the Age of Imperial Unity, the Classical Age,
  the Age of Imperial Kanauj); and Majumdar’s *Ancient India*.

- S. M. Srinivasa Chari, *Vaiṣṇavism: Its Philosophy, Theology and
  Religious Discipline*.

- Doris Srinivasan, *Many Heads, Arms and Eyes*.

- J. N. Banerjea, *The Development of Hindu Iconography*.

- Manasataramgini, “The roots of Vaiṣṇavam: a view from the numerology
  of Vedic texts.”

- **Colonial framing** (Part One): H. H. Wilson and A. Weber, as
  reported and refuted by Bhandarkar, R. P. Chanda, and H. C.
  Raychaudhuri, and summarized by Jaiswal in her opening chapter.
